name: Canencia Pass and Lozoya Valley Loop
name_original: Guadalix de la Sierra, Buitrago de Lozoya – ruta circular
description: This difficult 53-mile road cycling route from Guadalix de la Sierra to Buitrago de Lozoya offers challenging climbs and scenic views.
region_intro: Road cycling around Guadalix De La Sierra offers varied terrain within the Sierra de Guadarrama foothills, characterized by an extensive valley and surrounding hills. The region features a mix of flat stretches, undulating roads, and demanding climbs into higher elevations. Cyclists can find routes around the Embalse de Pedrezuela and through forests of cork oaks and holm oaks, providing diverse landscapes for road cycling. Best road cycling routes around Guadalix De La Sierra The most popular road cycling route is Guadalix de la Sierra, La Cabrera, Soto del Real – circular route, a 66.6 miles (107.2 km) trail that takes 5 hours 9 minutes to complete. This difficult route explores the local towns and countryside. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Canencia Pass and Lozoya Valley Loop, a difficult 53.0 miles (85.3 km) path. This route features significant elevation gain as it traverses a mountain pass and descends into the Lozoya Valley. Local road cyclists also love the El Vellón – View of El Atazar Reservoir loop from Guadalix de la Sierra, a 56.8 miles (91.4 km) trail leading through varied terrain with views of the El Atazar Reservoir, often completed in about 4 hours 15 minutes. Road cycling around Guadalix De La Sierra is defined by mountain passes of the Sierra de Guadarrama, scenic reservoir circuits, and routes through valleys and forests. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult ability levels. The routes in Guadalix De La Sierra are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.9 stars from over 40 reviews. More than 5000 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Guadalix De La Sierra's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain and difficulty can I expect on road cycling routes around Guadalix de la Sierra?
A: The region offers a diverse mix of terrain. You'll find flat stretches, undulating roads, and demanding climbs, especially as you ascend into the higher elevations of the Sierra de Guadarrama. Routes range from easy to difficult, with 9 moderate and 7 difficult routes available.

Q: Are there challenging mountain passes for experienced road cyclists?
A: Yes, Guadalix de la Sierra is situated at the foothills of the Sierra de Guadarrama, providing access to challenging mountain passes like Puerto de la Morcuera, Puerto de Navacerrada, and Puerto de Cotos. These are known for steep gradients and panoramic views. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e977470492/canencia-pass-and-lozoya-valley-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Canencia Pass and Lozoya Valley Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which features significant elevation gain.

Q: Are there any easier or moderate road cycling routes suitable for less experienced riders?
A: While many routes offer significant challenges, there are options for moderate ability levels. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39560775" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Manzanares el Real Castle – Santillana Reservoir loop from Guadalix de la Sierra&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate route that covers about 60 km with a more manageable elevation gain.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see while road cycling in the area?
A: You can enjoy scenic views of the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/484448" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pedrezuela Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2712180" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santillana Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;. The region is also characterized by forests of cork oaks, holm oaks, and junipers. A notable natural landmark is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6344971" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hervidero Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, a double waterfall on the Guadalix River.

Q: Are there many circular road cycling routes available?
A: Yes, many routes are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. A popular example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e977460664/guadalix-de-la-sierra-la-cabrera-soto-del-real-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guadalix de la Sierra, La Cabrera, Soto del Real – circular route&lt;/a&gt;, which is a challenging 107 km loop.

Q: When is the best time of year to go road cycling in Guadalix de la Sierra?
A: The region benefits from a characteristic microclimate with mild winters and warm summers, making it suitable for cycling for much of the year. Spring is particularly beautiful when wildflowers are in bloom, and shaded sections through forests offer relief during warmer months.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in Guadalix de la Sierra?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.9 stars from over 40 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, from challenging mountain passes to scenic reservoir circuits, and the well-maintained roads that wind through lush forests and high-mountain scenery.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available in Guadalix de la Sierra?
A: There are 17 road cycling routes available around Guadalix de la Sierra, catering to various skill levels from easy to difficult.

Q: Can I find routes with views of reservoirs?
A: Absolutely. Many routes offer stunning views of the local reservoirs. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10441890" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Vellón – View of El Atazar Reservoir loop from Guadalix de la Sierra&lt;/a&gt; is a great option, providing views of the El Atazar Reservoir.

Q: Are there any routes that pass by historical sites or cultural landmarks?
A: While the focus is on natural beauty, some routes may pass near historical points. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39560775" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Manzanares el Real Castle – Santillana Reservoir loop from Guadalix de la Sierra&lt;/a&gt; brings you close to Manzanares el Real, which is known for its castle.

Q: What is the average elevation gain for a typical road cycling route in this region?
A: The elevation gain varies significantly depending on the route's difficulty. Challenging routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e977460664/guadalix-de-la-sierra-la-cabrera-soto-del-real-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guadalix de la Sierra, La Cabrera, Soto del Real – circular route&lt;/a&gt; can have over 1400 meters of ascent, while moderate routes will have less.
tips:
The Castle and Walls of Buitrago del Lozoya, one of the most important medieval sites in the Community of Madrid:
The town has pre-Roman roots and was mentioned by Livy as a Celtiberian fortress (Litabrum).
Muslim Fortification: Between the 8th and 11th centuries, the Muslims built the wall to protect the territory.
Reconquest: Alfonso VI conquered it in 1085, and it passed into Christian hands.
Current Castle: Built in the 15th century by the Mendoza family, during the reign of John II of Castile.
Historical Uses: Noble residence, defensive stronghold, and setting for events such as the imprisonment of Juana la Beltraneja.
It is a huge viaduct located in the heart of the Lozoya valley. One of the most important engineering works in the entire Sierra Norte de Madrid. It has a length of 240 meters.

The port of Canencia is one of the most pleasant to climb in the Sierra de Guadarrama. On any of its slopes, both from Miraflores de la Sierra and from the town of Canencia, the slopes are not too steep and the landscape is incredible.
A popular and well-visited spring with clean, sweet water.
Climbing is climbing. Gets steeper as you get closer to Miraflores. Not many cars.

faqs.question: Is this circular route suitable for beginner road cyclists?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as difficult due to its significant elevation gain of approximately 1,280 meters and a distance of over 85 kilometers. It features long, consistent climbs and technical descents, demanding good fitness and experience with mountainous terrain. Beginners might find it very challenging.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is predominantly mountainous, characterized by the Sierra de Guadarrama range. You can expect well-paved roads, often narrow, with significant elevation changes including long climbs and descents. The scenery is diverse, passing through riverside forests, pine and oak groves, and offering views of the Lozoya Valley.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: The route offers several notable highlights. You'll pass by the historic Buitrago del Lozoya Castle and Walls, cross the impressive Taboada Viaduct, and ascend the scenic Canencia Pass. Other points include the Brezal Spring and another Viaduct Between Miraflores and Guadalix de la Sierra.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: You can typically find parking in the towns of Guadalix de la Sierra or Buitrago del Lozoya, which serve as excellent starting points for this circular route. Both towns are popular hubs for cyclists and offer amenities.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The region generally benefits from warm and sunny weather for much of the year, making spring and autumn ideal for cycling. These seasons offer pleasant temperatures and vibrant natural scenery. Summer can be hot, especially during midday, while winter might bring colder temperatures and potential for snow at higher elevations like Canencia Pass.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay an entrance fee to cycle in the Sierra de Guadarrama National Park?
faqs.answer: Generally, you do not need special permits or to pay an entrance fee for road cycling on public roads within the Sierra de Guadarrama National Park. However, always respect local regulations and signage, especially concerning protected areas. For specific details, you can consult official tourism sites like visitmadrid.es.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route itself is a road cycling path, dogs are generally not suitable companions for a strenuous 85 km road bike ride. If you plan to stop in towns, many establishments may have restrictions. It's best to leave your furry friends at home for this particular cycling adventure.

faqs.question: How can I reach the starting points of this route by public transport?
faqs.answer: Both Guadalix de la Sierra and Buitrago del Lozoya are accessible by public transport from Madrid, typically via bus services. Check local bus schedules for routes connecting to these towns, keeping in mind that transporting a bicycle on public transport might have specific rules or limitations.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route intersects with several other paths. Notably, it covers sections of the [CIMA MA03] Canencia * M-604 / Canencia, [CIMA MA03] Canencia * Miraflores, and the Camino de Santiago Complutense. You'll also find smaller portions overlapping with the Centro BTT "Valle del Lozoya" - Ruta 2 and the Senda del Genaro.

faqs.question: Are there any specific considerations for tackling the climbs on this route?
faqs.answer: Given the route's 'difficult' rating and significant elevation gain, be prepared for sustained climbs, particularly around the Canencia Pass. Pacing yourself, maintaining a steady rhythm, and ensuring you have appropriate gearing for steep gradients are key. The descents can be technical, so good bike handling skills are essential.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or get refreshments along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route passes through charming towns like Guadalix de la Sierra and Buitrago del Lozoya, which offer various options for cafes, restaurants, and shops where you can refuel and refresh. It's always a good idea to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially between towns.
